perturbation.original_text: 1.01 "BUSINESS" shall mean the design, manufacture, marketing, sale, leasing and distribution of health care products of the types and performing the functions of the <PAGE> 2 products designed, manufactured, marketed, sold, leased or distributed by Seller through the Division as of the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.01 "BUSINESS" shall mean activities generally related to the healthcare industry.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'BUSINESS' specifically referred to the design, manufacture, marketing, sale, leasing, and distribution of health care products. The modified definition broadens the scope to 'activities generally related to the healthcare industry.' This omission of specific activities creates an in-text contradiction because Section 2.03 refers to the original definition when discussing non-competition. The new vague definition makes the non-compete clause difficult to enforce, as it's unclear which activities are now prohibited.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The original definition of 'BUSINESS' included specific activities like design, manufacture, marketing, etc. The changed definition broadens it to 'activities generally related to the healthcare industry,' omitting the specific activities. Section 2.03 refers to the original definition when discussing non-competition, but the new definition is vague, making it unclear what activities are now prohibited and therefore creating a direct contradiction that makes enforcement unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 1.02 "COMPETE" OR "COMPETING" shall mean with respect to the Business: (i) the design, manufacture, marketing, sale, leasing or distribution of health care products of the types or performing the functions of the products designed, manufactured, marketed, sold, leased and distributed by Seller through the Division as of the date of this Agreement; (ii) hiring, soliciting or attempting to hire or solicit any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date either on Seller's behalf or on behalf of any other person or entity; or (iii) entering into or attempting to enter into any business substantially similar to the Business, either alone or with any individual, partnership, corporation or association; provided, however, that Competing shall not mean or include the ownership, disposition or use of Excluded Assets.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.02 "COMPETE" OR "COMPETING" shall mean activities the Seller agrees not to engage in. The specifics are outlined in Exhibit A.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'COMPETE' provided specific examples of prohibited activities. By replacing it with a vague reference to 'Exhibit A', which we assume does not exist in this perturbation, the contract creates an in-text contradiction. Section 2.03 relies on this definition, but because there are no specifics within the main agreement, the non-compete clause becomes unenforceable. This is because Section 2.03 references the 'compete' term, however, the details were supposedly provided in Exhibit A.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a specific definition of 'COMPETE'. The changed text replaces this with a vague reference to Exhibit A, which is assumed to not exist. This creates a contradiction with Section 2.03, which relies on the original definition to define the scope of the non-compete agreement. Without a clear definition of 'COMPETE', Section 2.03 becomes unenforceable, creating a strong in-text contradiction, as the definition essential for the enforceability of 2.03 has been removed. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret. Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees to maintain confidentiality as generally understood under common law principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original Section 2.02(a) provided a detailed definition of 'Confidential Information,' including specific examples. The modified version removes these specifics and replaces them with a vague reference to 'common law principles.' This omission creates an in-text contradiction because Section 2.02(b) still prohibits the use or disclosure of 'Confidential Information' for a specific period. Without the original definition, it's unclear what information is actually protected, making the clause difficult to enforce and thus, creating a contradiction between (a) and (b).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' with specific examples. The changed text replaces this definition with a vague reference to 'common law principles.' This directly contradicts section (b), which prohibits the use or disclosure of 'Confidential Information' without the specific definition provided in the original text, making it unclear what information is protected and rendering the clause difficult to enforce. This qualifies as a strong in-text contradiction under the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type as defined.

perturbation.original_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees to limit competitive activities following the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specified a three-year non-competition period and defined the geographic 'Territory.' The modified clause removes the time period and eliminates the geographic location of the clause, limiting the scope of the agreement. Although the non-compete clause still exists, the omission of the specific time and location parameters creates uncertainty and in effect, undermines the entire reason for the non-compete clause. It does not invalidate it in its entirety, however, the parties can claim now that the original intention was not agreed to. This directly impacts the remedies clause, since the definition of 'irreparable harm' becomes much harder to define.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to an injunction, without the necessity of posting any bond, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit three-year non-competition period and the definition of the 'Territory'. This directly contradicts section 2.05 REMEDIES, which relies on a clearly defined scope of Article II (including 2.03) to determine what constitutes a violation and 'irreparable harm.' Without the specific time and location parameters, it becomes difficult to enforce the remedies, as the scope of prohibited activity is now uncertain and the meaning of 'irreparable harm' is harder to define. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as the enforceability of the remedies clause is now undermined by the vagueness of the non-compete agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 1.03 "DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY" as they modify the words "Compete" or "Competing" shall mean: (i) acting as an agent, representative, consultant, or independent contractor of any entity or enterprise that is Competing with the Business; (ii) participating in any such Competing entity or enterprise as an owner, partner, limited partner, joint venturer, or stockholder (except as a stockholder owning less than a five percent interest in a corporation whose shares are actively traded on a national securities exchange or in the over-the-counter market); and (iii) communicating to any such Competing entity or enterprise the names or addresses or any other information concerning any past, present or identified prospective client, customer or supplier of the Division prior to Closing.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.03 "DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY" shall have its plain English meaning.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY' provided specific examples of prohibited actions. By replacing it with 'plain English meaning,' the contract introduces ambiguity. Section 2.03 relies on this definition to determine the scope of the non-competition agreement. This omission creates an in-text contradiction, as the phrase appears in the non-compete clause, but the meaning of it becomes questionable, since 'plain English' isn't a legal standard, and it can mean different things to different parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a specific definition for 'DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY,' which is then used in the non-compete clause (Section 2.03). The changed text replaces this definition with 'plain English meaning,' introducing ambiguity. This directly contradicts Section 2.03, which relies on a clear definition of 'DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY' to determine the scope of the non-competition agreement. Because 'plain English' is open to interpretation, this omission creates a strong contradiction making the enforceability of the non-compete clause questionable.

perturbation.original_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing 3 <PAGE> 4 Date, or request or induce any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing Date to terminate his or her employment and accept employment with another entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.04 NON-SOLICITATION AND NON-INTERFERENCE. Seller agrees not to solicit employees of the Purchasers.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause included a specific one-year time frame and prohibited both hiring and inducing employees to leave. The modified clause removes the time restriction and the explicit prohibition against inducing employees to terminate their employment. While the main agreement still mentions the non-solicitation provision, the lack of specifics causes an in-text contradiction, specifically with the Remedies provision of the document. If the Non-Solicitation clause is not well-defined, it is difficult to properly claim and calculate damages caused by the seller. Thus, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.05 REMEDIES. Seller acknowledges that any violation of this Article II will cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent and that damages are not an adequate remedy. Seller, therefore, agrees that Purchasers and Parent shall be entitled to an injunction, without the necessity of posting any bond, enjoining, prohibiting, and restraining Seller from the continuance of any such violation, in addition to any monetary recovery that might be available to Purchasers and Parent by reason of a violation of this Agreement and any other remedies at law or in equity, including without limitation specific performance.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the scope of the non-solicitation agreement by removing the one-year time frame and the explicit prohibition against inducing employees to terminate their employment. This creates an in-text contradiction because the Remedies section (2.05) anticipates a clear violation of 'Article II'. With the weakened Non-Solicitation clause (2.04), it becomes difficult to determine the extent of damages or enforce the remedies effectively, leading to a direct conflict with the Remedies provision, which relies on a well-defined non-solicitation agreement. The lack of specificity makes it difficult to properly claim and calculate damages, leading to a strong in-text contradiction based on the provided explanation and 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.
